mappings.rationaleAI: Nuclear physics covers fission, chain reactions, the Manhattan Project, and nuclear weapons, directly relevant to nuclear weapons including fission energy sources and blast/fallout effects.
The Manhattan Project covers the development of the first nuclear weapons, the Trinity test, and the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, directly relevant to nuclear weapons technology.
Directly covers the evolution of military strategy and tactics, the impact of technology on warfare from gunpowder to nuclear weapons, and the professionalization of armies.
Clausewitz's On War covers military theory including offensive and defensive warfare, the fog of war, friction, and the relationship between war and the state.
War in the 20th century covers the impact of industrial technology on warfare, nuclear weapons and deterrence theory, directly relevant to modern military technology.
The Battle of Lepanto (1571) was a major naval engagement involving oar and sail-powered warships, relevant to sail-powered surface ship weapon platforms.
The Battle of Crecy demonstrates the devastating effectiveness of the English longbow against cavalry, relevant to early hand-powered missile weapons and animal mounts as weapon platforms.
The Battle of Salamis was a decisive naval engagement involving oar-powered warships (galleys), relevant to oar-powered surface ship weapon platforms.
The Great Wall of China is a massive permanent fortification for defence against nomadic peoples, directly relevant to permanent fortification before gunpowder and strategic engineering support.
The Battle of Thermopylae demonstrates ancient hand-powered weapons and defensive tactics at a fortified pass, relevant to early weaponry and personal protection.
The neutron's significance for nuclear fission chain reactions is foundational to nuclear weapons energy sources.
Nuclear fusion discusses thermonuclear weapons alongside stellar nucleosynthesis, directly relevant to fusion as a nuclear weapon energy source.
The Spanish Armada demonstrates gunpowder-era naval warfare with sail-powered warships, relevant to gunpowder weapons and sail-powered weapon platforms.
Hadrian's Wall is a Roman frontier fortification with milecastles and forts, directly exemplifying permanent fortification before gunpowder and tactical/strategic engineering support.
The Siege of Malta involved the defence of permanent fortifications (forts) against Ottoman assault in the early gunpowder era.
The Siege of Vienna 1683 involved permanent fortification defence in the gunpowder era against Ottoman siege forces.
The Russo-Japanese War demonstrated modern industrial warfare including naval battles (Tsushima), relevant to steam-powered and internal combustion weapon platforms.
The Bronze Age collapse involves the military technologies of the era including bronze construction materials for weapons and the disruption caused by the 'Sea Peoples.'
The Battle of Lincoln 1217 involved siege warfare at Lincoln Castle, relevant to pre-gunpowder fortification and early hand-powered weapons.
The Shimabara Rebellion involved the siege of Hara Castle in the early gunpowder era, relevant to gunpowder weapons and permanent fortification.
The Wars of the Roses involved key battles with medieval weaponry and mounted cavalry, relevant to early hand-powered weapons and animal mounts.
